title: Entropy
definition: A measure of disorder, randomness, or uncertainty in a system. In thermodynamics, entropy quantifies the number of microscopic configurations corresponding to a macroscopic state. The second law states entropy of an isolated system never decreases.
formula: S = k_B ln(Ω); dS ≥ 0 for isolated systems

proofs.description: Boltzmann's entropy formula connects microscopic states to macroscopic entropy
proofs.reference: Boltzmann, L. (1877). Über die Beziehung zwischen dem zweiten Hauptsatze der mechanischen Wärmetheorie und der Wahrscheinlichkeitsrechnung.

proofs.description: Countless experiments confirm the second law; perpetual motion machines of the second kind impossible
proofs.reference: Carnot, S. (1824). Réflexions sur la puissance motrice du feu.

difficulty_levels.beginner: Entropy is a measure of disorder. Things naturally tend to become more disordered over time unless energy is added.
difficulty_levels.intermediate: Entropy quantifies the number of ways a system can be arranged microscopically while looking the same macroscopically. Higher entropy = more possible arrangements.
difficulty_levels.expert: Entropy bridges thermodynamics and information theory. Boltzmann's entropy (k_B ln Ω) counts microstates. Shannon entropy (H = -Σ p_i log p_i) measures information uncertainty. The second law reflects the tendency toward macrostates with overwhelmingly more microstates.

extra_fields.historical_context: Term coined by Rudolf Clausius in 1865 from Greek 'εντροπια' (transformation). Boltzmann's statistical interpretation (1877) revolutionized understanding.
extra_fields.limitations: Applies to large systems; fluctuations possible at small scales. Definition ambiguous for non-equilibrium systems.
